notes.anchor: it matters greatly to my standing that I reach the city as soon as possible
notes.note: Cicero is hurrying home from his Cilician governorship in late 50 BC, hoping to be voted a triumph; "the city" is Rome, which a commander seeking a triumph could not enter until the vote, but which he needed to reach before the political crisis deepened.

notes.anchor: I have sent Marion to you
notes.note: Mario (Greek "Marion") is a slave-courier of Cicero's household, shuttled between Cicero and the sick Tiro to carry letters.

notes.anchor: stay on a little at Patrae
notes.note: Patrae, a port in Achaea, is where Cicero left the ailing Tiro in the care of local friends; the whole of book 16's opening run of letters concerns his recovery there.

notes.anchor: you will catch us at Leucas
notes.note: Leucas, an island town off Acarnania, was the next stage on Cicero's homeward sea route; Tiro could rejoin the party there if he sailed at once.

notes.anchor: The third day before the Nones
notes.note: A Roman calendar date, counted inclusively backward from the Nones (here the Nones of November, the 5th); the third day before is thus 3 November 50 BC.
